prompt: When transferring a resident from bed to wheelchair, the NA should first:

options.text: Lock the wheelchair brakes and ensure nonskid footwear if used
Pull the resident by the arms quickly
Leave the bed at the highest position without explanation
Skip explaining the transfer to save time

explanation: Brakes, footwear, bed height, and clear communication prevent falls.

prompt: The safest way to turn a dependent resident in bed is to:

options.text: Twist only the shoulders forcefully
Use draw sheets/assistance and keep the spine in alignment per care plan
Pull by the head of the bed alone
Ask another resident to yank the legs

explanation: Logrolling/assistance protects skin and musculoskeletal injury.

prompt: A gait belt is used to:

options.text: Restrain a resident in a chair for staff convenience
Lift a resident by the belt alone from the floor after a fall without assessment
Provide a secure hold to assist ambulation/transfers when appropriate
Replace the need for footwear

explanation: Gait belts aid safe mobility; they are not punitive restraints.

prompt: Oral care for an unconscious resident typically includes:

options.text: Giving large gulps of water
Skipping mouth care because they cannot ask
Brushing while fully flat supine with pooling water
Positioning on the side, using swabs per protocol, and protecting the airway

explanation: Aspiration precautions guide unconscious oral hygiene.

prompt: When dressing a resident with a weak right arm, the NA should:

options.text: Put the weaker arm into clothing first and remove it last
Force the weak arm last into tight sleeves roughly
Leave the resident undressed for privacy of staff
Dress the strong side first always as the only rule

explanation: Weak side first when dressing; opposite when undressing—reduces pain/strain.

distractorExplanations.d: Strong side first is the undressing pattern.

prompt: To prevent pressure injuries, the NA should:

options.text: Massage reddened bony areas vigorously
Reposition immobile residents at least every 2 hours (or per care plan) and keep skin clean/dry
Leave wet linens to “air dry” on the skin
Keep the head of bed at 90° continuously for all residents

explanation: Pressure relief and moisture control are core prevention strategies.

distractorExplanations.d: High Fowler’s increases shear for some; follow plan.

prompt: A resident is sliding down in bed. The NA should:

options.text: Drag by the armpits alone
Yank the ankles
Use proper lift/assist techniques and enough help; avoid dragging the skin
Ignore skin shearing risk

explanation: Shearing contributes to pressure injuries; use draw sheets and teamwork.

prompt: Perineal care should be performed:

options.text: From back to front for females routinely
Without gloves
With the curtain open to the hallway
From front to back for females, using clean areas of the washcloth, maintaining privacy and dignity

explanation: Front-to-back reduces UTI risk; standard precautions and privacy apply.

prompt: If a resident starts to fall while ambulating, the NA should:

options.text: Control the fall and lower the resident gently to the floor protecting the head, then call for help
Try to catch and carry them alone upright at all costs
Step away so they fall freely
Immediately apply a restraint

explanation: Safe fall techniques reduce injury to resident and NA; then assess and report.

distractorExplanations.d: Restraints are not the fall response.

prompt: Range-of-motion exercises should be:

options.text: Forced past the point of severe pain
Performed as ordered, supporting joints, stopping if pain occurs, and reporting changes
Skipped permanently if the resident is tired once
Done only on the neck with rapid circling for all residents

explanation: ROM preserves mobility; never force through pain; follow care plan.

distractorExplanations.d: Neck ROM is cautious/specific—not vigorous for all.

prompt: A bedpan should be offered:

options.text: While visitors remain at the bedside without asking the resident
Without gloves if the NA is “used to it”
With privacy, proper positioning, and hand hygiene before/after
Only once per day regardless of need

explanation: Elimination assistance requires dignity and infection control.

prompt: When making an occupied bed, the NA should:

options.text: Expose the entire body to the room
Leave the resident on the edge without support
Shake dirty linen in the air in the room
Keep the resident covered for privacy/warmth and use side rails per policy for safety during turning

explanation: Occupied bedmaking maintains safety, dignity, and infection control.

prompt: Adaptive devices for eating (e.g., built-up utensils) are used to:

options.text: Promote independence for residents with limited grip or coordination
Punish slow eaters
Replace all feeding assistance needs automatically without assessment
Allow staff to skip mealtime monitoring for dysphagia risk

explanation: Restorative care supports function; still follow dysphagia precautions.

prompt: Before helping a resident into the shower, the NA should:

options.text: Use the hottest water available
Check water temperature (safely warm), ensure grab bars/nonslip surfaces, and stay within safety guidelines
Leave a confused resident alone behind a locked door
Ignore wet floors

explanation: Burns and falls are major shower risks.

prompt: Elastic stockings (TED hose) should be applied:

options.text: Over open draining ulcers without nursing direction
So tight that toes are blue and numb without reporting
On clean dry legs, without wrinkles, as ordered—usually in the morning
Inside out with large wrinkles at the ankles

explanation: Wrinkles and incorrect fit impair circulation; report skin/neurovascular changes.

distractorExplanations.b: Cyanosis/numbness = remove per protocol and report.

prompt: Standard precautions mean the NA should:

options.text: Use hand hygiene and appropriate PPE for all residents as indicated
Only wash hands if visibly soiled monthly
Share razors between residents to save cost
Skip gloves for blood contact if in a hurry

explanation: Treat all blood/body fluids as potentially infectious.

distractorExplanations.c: Never share personal care items that risk blood exposure.

prompt: The most important measure to prevent infection spread is:

options.text: Wearing jewelry in the sink
Hand hygiene
Using the resident’s toothbrush on another person
Leaving bedpans uncleaned

explanation: Hand hygiene is the single most effective infection-control practice.

prompt: A normal adult oral temperature is approximately:

options.text: 105°F as normal baseline

explanation: Know facility normal ranges and report fever/hypothermia promptly.

distractorExplanations.d: 82°F is incompatible with normal oral temps.

prompt: When counting respirations, the NA should:

options.text: Ask them to breathe as fast as possible
Count for 2 seconds and multiply by 100
Only count if the resident is talking continuously
Count without telling the resident you are counting breaths, after pulse, noting rate/effort

explanation: Awareness alters breathing; observe rise/fall for 30–60 seconds per policy.

distractorExplanations.b: Too short an interval is inaccurate.
distractorExplanations.c: Talking makes counting hard—wait if needed.

prompt: A radial pulse is taken at the:

options.text: Wrist on the thumb side
Neck only
Behind the knee only
Temple only

explanation: Radial is the common NA pulse site; report irregular/weak pulses.

distractorExplanations.b: Carotid is neck—used in emergencies carefully.

prompt: Which blood pressure reading should be reported immediately as abnormally high for most adults?

options.text: 110/70 mm Hg in a stable adult
188/110 mm Hg in a resident who is symptomatic or per facility thresholds
118/76 mm Hg
100/60 mm Hg in a baseline low-normal resident

explanation: Know resident baselines and facility report parameters; severe hypertension needs prompt nursing notification.

prompt: Droplet precautions may require the NA to:

options.text: Never wear a mask for influenza-like illness when policy requires it
Leave the door open with no signage when isolation is ordered
Wear a mask when working within close range of the resident as directed
Share drinking cups

explanation: Follow the isolation sign and nurse instructions for PPE.

prompt: Dirty linen should be:

options.text: Placed on the floor temporarily
Used to wipe spills then returned to the cart
Taken home to wash
Held away from the uniform and placed in appropriate bags; never shaken

explanation: Linen handling prevents environmental contamination.

prompt: A pulse oximeter reading is affected when:

options.text: Nail polish, poor perfusion, or motion interferes with the sensor
The resident has clean warm fingers and still sensor placement only as a problem always
Hand hygiene is performed correctly as a false low cause
Privacy curtains are closed

explanation: Report unexpected SpO2 values and recheck technique/site.

prompt: Before measuring weight, the NA should:

options.text: Estimate weight to save time always
Balance/zero the scale, use consistent clothing/footwear approach, and assist for safety
Weigh while the resident holds a full suitcase undocumented
Skip documenting the value

explanation: Accurate weights guide clinical decisions (heart failure, nutrition).

prompt: Hand hygiene with alcohol-based rub is appropriate when:

options.text: Hands are covered in feces
Caring for a resident with C. difficile and soap/water is required by policy—you use only alcohol without washing
Hands are not visibly soiled and facility policy allows it
After touching blood with bare hands without washing opportunity—skip both

explanation: Soap/water for visible soil and spore-forming organisms per policy (e.g., C. diff).

distractorExplanations.d: Blood exposure needs immediate washing and report.

prompt: When removing PPE, the general principle is:

options.text: Touch your face with dirty gloves freely
Reuse gloves between residents by rinsing with water only
Tie used gowns on the overbed table for the next shift
Remove the most contaminated items first in the order taught, and perform hand hygiene

explanation: Correct doffing prevents self-contamination.

prompt: A respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min in an adult resident is:

options.text: Too low (bradypnea)—report promptly
A normal finding for all adults
Too high (tachypnea)
Impossible to measure

explanation: Normal adult RR is often ~12–20; 8 needs nursing evaluation especially with sedation/opioids.

prompt: Catheter care by the NA includes:

options.text: Raising the drainage bag above the bladder routinely
Cleaning the peri area and catheter tubing as directed, keeping the bag below bladder level, and securing the catheter
Disconnecting the closed system to rinse with tap water daily
Using the catheter bag as a handle to reposition the resident

explanation: Closed system and dependent drainage reduce UTI risk.

distractorExplanations.c: Do not break closed systems improperly.

prompt: If a sterile dressing pack is opened and left unattended in a dirty area, the NA should:

options.text: Use it anyway to avoid waste
Blow on it to “re-sterilize”
Consider it contaminated and obtain new supplies per procedure
Cover with a bath towel and proceed as sterile

explanation: Sterility is absolute—when in doubt, replace.

distractorExplanations.a: Waste is secondary to infection risk.
distractorExplanations.d: Towels are not sterile barriers for that purpose.

prompt: Therapeutic communication includes:

options.text: Listening, clarifying, and allowing time for the resident to express feelings
Changing the subject whenever emotions appear
Telling residents their feelings are silly
Talking only about the NA’s personal problems

explanation: Empathy and presence support psychosocial well-being.

prompt: When a resident with dementia asks for her deceased husband, a helpful approach is often to:

options.text: Force detailed confrontation every time that he is dead
Respond with empathy and use redirection/validation techniques per care plan rather than repeated harsh corrections
Ignore and walk away silently always
Laugh at the question

explanation: Validation/redirection can reduce distress; follow the care plan and nurse guidance.

prompt: Cultural sensitivity means the NA should:

options.text: Impose the NA’s beliefs as the only correct ones
Refuse interpreters when language barriers exist
Respect preferences about food, touch, privacy, and end-of-life customs within facility rules
Mock religious items

explanation: Person-centered care includes cultural humility.

prompt: A resident is crying after a difficult phone call. The NA should:

options.text: Say “stop crying, it’s not a big deal”
Leave without acknowledgment
Share the story at the nurses’ station gossip circle
Sit with the resident, offer presence, and ask if they want to talk or have the nurse notified

explanation: Emotional support and confidentiality are part of psychosocial care.

prompt: Sexuality in older adults should be:

options.text: Respected as a normal human need within consent, privacy, and safety policies
Treated as shameful and mocked
Ignored even when inappropriate public behavior needs redirection
Used as joke material with other residents

explanation: Address inappropriate behavior professionally; support privacy for appropriate consensual expression per policy.

distractorExplanations.c: Public inappropriate acts need calm redirection.

prompt: To help a hearing-impaired resident, the NA should:

options.text: Shout into the bad ear from behind
Face the resident, speak clearly at a moderate pace, reduce background noise, and use assistive devices
Chew gum and look away while talking
Avoid written cues when they help

explanation: Communication adaptations reduce isolation.

prompt: Residents with vision loss benefit when the NA:

options.text: Rearranges furniture often without telling them
Leaves doors half-open as trip hazards without warning
Identifies self when entering, explains care before touching, and keeps pathways clear
Avoids describing food locations on the tray

explanation: Consistency and verbal cueing support independence and safety.

prompt: Agnosia, aphasia, and apraxia are terms often related to:

options.text: Normal vital sign values
Types of wheelchairs only
Laundry cycles
Neurologic changes that affect recognition, language, and skilled movement—requiring patience and adapted communication

explanation: Understanding cognitive/communication deficits improves care and reduces agitation.

prompt: When family members are upset about care, the NA should:

options.text: Listen respectfully and involve the nurse/supervisor for concerns beyond the NA role
Argue loudly in the hallway
Promise medical outcomes you cannot control
Discuss other residents’ charts to compare

explanation: Service recovery and escalation maintain trust; stay in scope.

prompt: Loneliness in long-term care may be reduced by:

options.text: Leaving the TV on at max volume 24/7 without preference
Encouraging appropriate social activities and meaningful conversation
Restricting all visitors as default punishment
Ignoring the resident’s preferred hobbies

explanation: Psychosocial well-being is a care goal, not optional.

prompt: A resident refuses a bath. The best initial NA response is:

options.text: Force scrubbing against their will
Withhold meals as punishment
Ask about concerns, offer choices (time/type), and report refusal per policy without forcing
Tell them they are childish

explanation: Consent and dignity apply; negotiate and escalate to the nurse.

prompt: Reminiscence can be helpful because it:

options.text: Always worsens dementia instantly
Is forbidden in nursing homes
Replaces all medical treatment
Supports identity, mood, and cognitive engagement for many older adults

explanation: Life review activities are common psychosocial interventions.

prompt: If a resident makes a sexually inappropriate comment, the NA should:

options.text: Set a clear respectful boundary, state the behavior is inappropriate, and report per policy
Respond with flirtation
Shame the resident publicly at mealtime
Ignore repeated harassment without reporting

explanation: Protect yourself and maintain professionalism; facilities have reporting pathways.

prompt: Person-centered care emphasizes:

options.text: Identical rigid routines for every person with no exceptions ever
Knowing the resident’s preferences, routines, and life story to guide daily care
Staff convenience as the only schedule driver
Ignoring advance directives

explanation: Individualization improves outcomes and satisfaction.

prompt: Anxiety before a procedure may be reduced when the NA:

options.text: Withholds all information to “avoid worry”
Uses threats to gain cooperation
Explains what will happen in simple terms and stays calm and supportive
Performs care without saying a word while startling the resident

explanation: Information and calm presence reduce fear.

prompt: Tasks outside the NA’s scope of practice include:

options.text: Giving medications (unless state-specific specially trained roles apply) and sterile invasive procedures not delegated legally
Assisting with ADLs
Measuring vital signs as assigned
Making occupied beds

explanation: NAs do not practice nursing/medicine beyond training and legal delegation.

prompt: If asked to do something beyond training, the NA should:

options.text: Attempt it secretly
Refuse politely and explain, then notify the nurse/supervisor
Ask another NA who is also untrained to do it
Document that it was done when it was not

explanation: Refusal of unsafe assignments protects residents and licensure/certification.

prompt: Residents’ rights include:

options.text: Being punished by withholding mail
Having personal funds taken by staff
Privacy, dignity, freedom from abuse/restraints (except proper orders), and participation in care decisions
Forced silence about grievances

explanation: OBRA and facility policies protect resident rights in nursing homes.

prompt: An example of neglect is:

options.text: Answering a call light promptly
Reporting a skin tear immediately
Offering toileting assistance
Failing to provide needed care such as turning, fluids, or hygiene that results in harm risk

explanation: Neglect is failure to provide goods/services necessary to avoid harm.

prompt: If you suspect abuse, you should:

options.text: Report immediately through the required chain/hotline per facility and state law
Wait months to be sure
Confront the abuser alone violently
Post accusations on social media with names

explanation: NAs are mandatory reporters in healthcare settings; timely reporting is required.

distractorExplanations.c: Do not escalate personally with violence.

prompt: Confidentiality means:

options.text: Discussing residents by name in elevators with visitors present
Sharing resident information only with those who need it to provide care
Posting wound photos online
Leaving charts open in public

explanation: HIPAA and ethics protect PHI.

prompt: A restraint may be used only when:

options.text: Applied for staff convenience during breaks
Used to punish wandering
Ordered by a provider, used as a last resort with least restriction, and monitored per policy
Tied tightly without checking circulation

explanation: Improper restraint use is abuse; know release/check requirements.

prompt: Accurate documentation should be:

options.text: Written for tomorrow about today’s care you forgot, inventing details
Full of diagnoses the NA invents
Erased with white-out without following error policy
Objective, timely, legible/electronic-correct, and free of opinions/blame language

explanation: If it wasn’t documented, it is hard to prove it was done—never fabricate.

distractorExplanations.b: NAs record observations, not medical diagnoses.

prompt: Call lights should be:

options.text: Answered promptly and placed within the resident’s reach
Unplugged to reduce noise
Answered only at end of shift
Given only to private-pay residents

explanation: Access to assistance is a safety and rights issue.

prompt: An Ombudsman is:

options.text: The laundry supervisor only
An advocate who helps residents with complaints and rights issues
A type of antibiotic
The NA’s personal attorney automatically

explanation: Residents can contact the ombudsman; staff must not retaliate.

prompt: HIPAA training matters for NAs because they:

options.text: Never see any health information
Are exempt from all privacy laws
Handle PHI daily and can cause breaches through talk, records, or devices
May sell resident lists for bonuses

explanation: Workforce members are accountable for privacy practices.

prompt: A resident offers the NA money as a thank-you. The NA should:

options.text: Accept cash secretly
Ask for more
Accept and not report if policy forbids it
Politely refuse gifts of money per policy and thank them verbally

explanation: Gift policies prevent exploitation allegations; follow facility rules.

prompt: Chain of command is used to:

options.text: Report problems to the appropriate supervisor so issues are addressed safely
Skip the nurse and only call the newspaper first
Hide errors
Assign blame publicly on social media

explanation: Use internal reporting pathways; emergencies still call for immediate help.

distractorExplanations.b: Media is not first-line clinical reporting.

prompt: Abandonment can occur when the NA:

options.text: Takes a scheduled break after proper handoff
Leaves an assigned shift/residents without giving report or being relieved
Calls for help during an emergency
Refuses an unsafe task and stays to cover other duties

explanation: Leaving without relief endangers residents.

distractorExplanations.d: Unsafe task refusal with continued coverage differs from walking out.

prompt: Objective observation example:

options.text: “Resident is lazy and faking”
“Resident is nasty”
“Resident ate 50% of lunch; stated abdominal pain 6/10; vomited 100 mL green fluid.”
“Resident wants attention like always”

explanation: Chart facts and quotes; avoid judgmental labels.
